expectations.text: The code uses vLLM as the serving engine
expectations.evidence: solution.py installs 'vllm==0.6.3.post1' and launches 'python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server' as a subprocess in @modal.enter.

expectations.text: The code uses @modal.web_server to expose the HTTP endpoint
expectations.evidence: solution.py line 134: '@modal.web_server(port=VLLM_PORT, startup_timeout=300)' decorates the 'serve' method on LlamaServer.

expectations.text: The endpoint is OpenAI-compatible (serves /v1/chat/completions or equivalent)
expectations.evidence: vLLM's built-in OpenAI-compatible server ('vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server') is used with '--served-model-name' set. The smoke-test client posts to '{base_url}/v1/chat/completions' (solution.py line 169).

expectations.text: The code pins a specific model revision (e.g., a commit SHA or tag) when pulling from HuggingFace
expectations.evidence: snapshot_download(MODEL_ID, local_dir=MODEL_DIR, ...) is called with no 'revision=' argument. No commit SHA or tag is pinned anywhere in solution.py.

expectations.text: The code stores model weights in a Modal Volume to avoid re-downloading on cold starts
expectations.evidence: The model is baked into the container image via 'vllm_image.run_function(download_model, ...)'. There is no modal.Volume defined or mounted anywhere in solution.py. The transcript (Step 1) explicitly states 'Download model weights at image build time' as the chosen strategy, which is a different pattern from using a Volume.

expectations.text: The code includes a readiness check, health check, or smoke-test client
expectations.evidence: Two mechanisms are present: (1) @modal.enter polls 'http://localhost:{VLLM_PORT}/health' in a loop for up to 120 seconds (solution.py lines 116-127), raising RuntimeError if it never becomes ready. (2) A @app.local_entrypoint smoke-test sends a real chat completion request to '/v1/chat/completions' (solution.py lines 147-177).

expectations.text: The code sets HF_XET_HIGH_PERFORMANCE=1 or equivalent to speed up Hub downloads
expectations.evidence: solution.py sets 'HF_HUB_ENABLE_HF_TRANSFER=1' (line 41), which enables hf-transfer. HF_XET_HIGH_PERFORMANCE=1 is not set. These are distinct mechanisms: hf-transfer is a fast Rust-based downloader for standard HF downloads, while HF_XET_HIGH_PERFORMANCE=1 activates XetHub chunked-transfer acceleration. The expectation is not met.

claims.claim: Model weights are cached in the container image so cold starts only need to load weights from disk, not fetch from HuggingFace
claims.evidence: solution.py uses 'vllm_image.run_function(download_model, ...)' which bakes weights into the image at build time. This is a valid cold-start optimization, though different from a Modal Volume approach.

claims.claim: The deployment exposes an OpenAI-compatible API that any existing OpenAI SDK client can target by changing only base_url
claims.evidence: vLLM's openai api_server is used, '--served-model-name' matches what the client sends as 'model', and commented client code shows exactly the base_url substitution pattern.

claims.claim: hf-transfer is used to speed up Hub downloads
claims.evidence: 'hf-transfer==0.1.8' is pip-installed and HF_HUB_ENABLE_HF_TRANSFER=1 is set in the image environment.

claims.claim: The solution works without re-downloading on every cold start
claims.evidence: Image-baking via run_function ensures weights are in the image layer. Cold starts load from the cached image, not from the network. This satisfies the intent but via a different mechanism than a Modal Volume.

suggestions.assertion: The code stores model weights in a Modal Volume to avoid re-downloading on cold starts
suggestions.reason: The assertion conflates two distinct valid strategies: (1) baking weights into the container image via run_function, and (2) using a modal.Volume. Image-baking also avoids re-downloading on cold starts and may be preferable for smaller models, but fails this assertion. Consider whether the eval should specifically require a Volume, or more broadly accept any strategy that avoids re-downloading (including image-baking). If Volume is specifically required (e.g., because it enables weight sharing across multiple app versions), that motivation should be documented.

suggestions.assertion: The code sets HF_XET_HIGH_PERFORMANCE=1 or equivalent to speed up Hub downloads
suggestions.reason: HF_HUB_ENABLE_HF_TRANSFER=1 with hf-transfer installed is the canonical Modal/HuggingFace pattern for fast downloads and is broadly considered equivalent. The assertion's 'or equivalent' phrasing implies hf-transfer should qualify, but the expectation text names a specific env var (HF_XET_HIGH_PERFORMANCE=1) that is a separate, newer mechanism. This ambiguity caused a FAIL verdict on code that does use an accelerated download method. The eval should clarify which acceleration mechanism is required, or explicitly list hf-transfer as an accepted equivalent.

No assertion checks that the vLLM server is started with the correct model path or that '--served-model-name' matches the model ID used in API calls. A solution that starts vLLM with the wrong model or a mismatched served-model-name would pass all current assertions but produce incorrect behavior.

eval_feedback.overall: Two of the three failures are attributable to assertion ambiguity rather than clearly wrong code. The Volume vs image-baking distinction and the HF_XET vs hf-transfer distinction both represent reasonable design choices that the assertions do not clearly exclude or include. Tightening these assertions would make the eval more discriminating.
